title: Generalised simultaneous transmission of arbitrary quantum states and classical information
abstract: We present a protocol which allows for arbitrary optical quantum states to simultaneously carry and transmit classical data, without sacrificing the integrity of either the quantum or classical information. Our scheme encodes classical information via displacements in the phase space prior to transmission and retrieves each classical symbol via a Gaussian continuous-variable teleportation. The original quantum state is then restored by guessing the the original displacement and performing the appropriate inverse operation. In the limit of sufficiently high classical signal and high squeezing, we show that our scheme is capable of perfectly reconstructing both the input classical signal and the input quantum state without loss of coherence. An example is given in terms of the transmission of a dual-rail Bell state.
summary: This paper details a Continuous-Variable Quantum Communication (CMQC) protocol enabling the simultaneous, low-error transmission of arbitrary quantum states and classical data. By encoding classical information via phase-space displacements and using continuous-variable teleportation, the scheme demonstrates high fidelity reconstruction. The results show that the protocol is robust, maintaining quantum correlations strong enough to violate the Bell inequality even under realistic channel losses.
main_problem: To develop a protocol for the simultaneous transmission of arbitrary quantum states and classical information over a single quantum channel without losing the integrity of either information type.
main_result: The protocol can achieve perfect reconstruction of both the classical signal and the quantum state in the limits of high classical signal strength and high squeezing, and it can violate the CHSH inequality under realistic conditions.
method: The scheme uses continuous-variable quantum teleportation combined with classical phase-space displacements for encoding and subsequent inverse displacements for decoding.
model_or_system: The system models optical quantum states transmitted through a lossy bosonic channel, utilizing continuous-variable teleportation and encoding classical data via coherent-state displacements.
key_observables: Symbol-Error Rate ($e_S$), CHSH expectation value ($S$), and the output state ($\hat{ho}_{	ext{out}}$).
important_parameters: Transmissivity ($\eta$), squeezing parameter ($r$), and classical signal strength ($\alpha$).
main_assumptions: The protocol assumes the ability to probabilistically infer the true classical displacement and relies on the channel being a pure-loss channel for some derivations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the protocol structure, the dependence of the CHSH value ($S$) on channel loss ($\eta$) for varying classical signal strength ($\alpha$) and squeezing ($r$), and the success probabilities ($p_s$) for post-selection.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CMQC protocol, analyzes its effective channel map in various limits (high SNR, infinite squeezing), and rigorously tests its capability to maintain quantum correlations by calculating the CHSH value for transmitted Bell states.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses quantum networking by proposing a robust method to multiplex classical data and quantum entanglement over one physical channel, which is crucial for future quantum internet architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Diagram showing the CMQC protocol (a) as well as the teleportation circuit ˆAtele (b). The N-mode state ˆρ Alice wishes to transmit is passed through the displacement operator ˆD(α), which encodes N classical symbols by displac- ing each arm in the phase space according to some coherent- state classical protocol. The displaced state is then trans- mitted over the N-mode channel EN to Bob. Bob teleports each mode of the displaced transmitted state ˆρ′ by mixing the incoming mode with one half of an EPR state of squeez- ing r on a balanced beamsplitter, then performing a dual homodyne measurement and correcting the output state via the measurement-dependent unitary operation...

Figure 2. CHSH criterion Sp as a function of channel loss η and classical signal strength α, evaluated on ρens. At each point, Sp is optimised over Bob’s EPR resource squeezing r. Post-selecting the correlations based on successful photon counts in principle permits the protocol to saturate the upper bound of Sp = 2 √

Figure 3. Diagram showing the transmission of a dual-rail Bell state ϕ− ϕ− via the CMQC protocol. Each half of the dual- rail state is encoded in the modes A1, A2 and A1s, A2s respectively, where the logical states are given by |0⟩L1 = |1⟩A1 |0⟩A2, |1⟩L1 = |0⟩A1 |1⟩A2, |0⟩L2 = |1⟩A1s |0⟩A2s and |1⟩L2 = |0⟩A1s |1⟩A2s .The half corresponding to the logical mode L2 is sent by Alice to Bob, where it is teleported onto the modes B1, B2 such that the total output of the protocol becomes an approximate equivalent dual-rail Bell state over the modes A1, A2 and B1, B2.

Figure 4. (a) Numerical evaluation of the CHSH criterion S for an instance of the CMQC protocol where the resource state is the dual-rail Bell state ˆρ = ϕ− ϕ− and the channels are identical pure-loss channels of transmissivity η. S is plotted as a function of the loss η for the case where the classical signal strength α = 1 (dash-dotted lines) and α = 10 (solid lines). The squeezing r of the teleporter resource EPR state is varied between multiple values representing feasible experimental parameters (e.g. [45–50]. For sufficiently high signal strength, as teleporter squeezing increases S begins to approach the upper bound Sη = 2 √

Figure 5. Success probabilities (left) and optimal rates (right) for the postselected protocols shown in Fig. 2 of the main text. The rate at which Bob obtains the correct measurement results with which to evaluate S is given trivially by the probability of success ps. For each loss η, the postselected quantity Sp = S/ps is optimised over the two-mode squeezing of Bob’s EPR resource r between r = 0 and 20 dB (r ∈[0, 2.3]). The optimal strategy for protocols with a high classical signal α and so high classical SNR is the obvious one, i.e. r −→∞.
